analysis.briefing_paragraph:
Harbour Town is one of the most honest course-fit tests on the Tour calendar, and I genuinely look forward to it every year because the pretenders get exposed fast. This is a TIGHT, COMPRESSED-distance track where the premium is almost entirely on accuracy off the tee, approach play into small, firm greens, and short game when you inevitably miss. ARG is the dominant stat here — not raw ball-striking, not distance, not even pure SG:APP in isolation. It's the combination of finding fairways, controlling trajectory and angle into elevated or guarded greens, and scrambling when the angle punishes you. Historical driving accuracy at Harbour Town runs around 68% — well above tour average — which means bombers who sacrifice accuracy are actively penalized, not just neutral. Distance multiplier is COMPRESSED, meaning long hitters get essentially no structural edge. The rough penalty is HIGH and the angle penalty is HIGH — miss the fairway here and you're not just in a bad lie, you're staring at a completely different shot shape requirement. Course management is a real differentiator. This is one of the few events where I actually upgrade the smart, shorter, accurate players and fade the distance guys who can't hit fairways. Webb Simpson famously dominated this venue for years — the archetype is clear. Weather data is not yet in, but wind relevance at Harbour Town is MODERATE. It's a coastal track on Hilton Head and can absolutely get breezy — any sustained wind tightens the course even further and adds another layer to the approach premium. No weather numbers yet, so I'll hold full wind reads for the midweek update. A few player narratives worth flagging now: Collin Morikawa is the first name I'm writing on the card conceptually — EXTRA CONFIRM PLUS at approach-dominant accuracy courses, full stop, and this is one of the best examples of that archetype on the schedule. I need to confirm his recent form and health before committing, but course fit is as strong as it gets. Matt Fitzpatrick is another name who should show up well here — accuracy driver, elite iron play, exactly the profile Harbour Town rewards. On the fallen star watch, Viktor Hovland's EXTRA CONFIRM status triggers at 40/1+ and I need to check his current form — if the ball-striking has stabilized at all, Harbour Town could be a bounce-back spot given the reduced distance penalty. The field notes are relatively clean: this is a signature event so it draws well, but the TIGHT course will naturally eliminate a chunk of the field by Round 2. No designated-event red flags jumping out. Stat data came in very thin this week — only 2/50 players matched — so I'm working heavily from framework designations, course fit, and career profiles. That's noted prominently and will be resolved when full odds and data arrive Monday. ---

[Odds note Mon 10:11PM ET] I'm seeing an empty current rankings array `[]` fed in as my pre-odds analysis. **I have no prior course/form analysis to layer odds onto.** This means I'm working without the foundational ball-striking profiles, recent form windows, course-fit assessments, and SG breakdowns that the framework requires before odds even enter the conversation.

**What I can do here:** Give you the odds structure analysis, flag pricing anomalies, and identify where the market is likely soft — but I want to be transparent that any "value" calls below are based on general Harbour Town knowledge and these players' publicly known profiles, NOT a completed framework analysis. The full bet card should be treated as directional, not final, until the form/course analysis is run and merged.

**Scheffler at +380 (20.8% implied)** is doing heavy lifting at the top. That's a steep chalk price for a field with genuine contenders. The book is essentially saying 1-in-5 chance for the world #1, which historically at Harbour Town is aggressive — this course has bitten Scheffler before, it rewards precision and local knowledge over raw dominance.

[Claude] ## HARBOUR TOWN — WHAT WINS HERE (Framework Anchor)
[Claude] Before I touch a single price, let me re-establish what the course demands, because that's what determines whether a price is fair.
[Claude] **Harbour Town is a precision test, not a power test.** It's one of the shortest tracks on tour. The premium is on:
[Claude] - **Approach Play (APP)** — small, firm, elevated greens demand precise distance control and trajectory
[Claude] - **Driving Accuracy (OTT)** — not distance, *accuracy*. Trees and tight corridors punish crooked drivers
[Claude] - **Around the Green / Short Game** — scoring zones are tight, par saves matter
[Claude] - **Putting on Bentgrass** — the one track where putting variance matters a bit more than usual because the greens are readable and consistent, rewarding elite putters

[Claude] **Ball-striking is still the engine here, but the profile shifts toward control over power.** Big hitters who can't shape it or hit fairways get exposed. Accurate, approach-dominant players with functional putting are the archetypal Harbour Town winner.
[Claude] Past winners profile: Dustin Johnson (accuracy phase), Bryson (control year), Stewart Cink, Jim Furyk repeatedly — this course loves precision.

[Claude] Conners is a perennial Harbour Town fit — elite GIR, doesn't need the putter to bail him out, keeps it in play. +6000 is a price that consistently undervalues him at courses like this. This is exactly the type of quiet value the market produces when there's no narrative around a player.
